serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese fried noodles with chicken is approximately 350 g, typically served as a single-plate main meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chow mein noodles with chicken, stir-fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA composite estimate for stir-fried noodle dish with chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for Burmese-style fried noodles with chicken. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for stir-fried noodles with chicken (chow mein analogue, NDB 18240 / FDC ID 172134) and FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle dish composites. Macros adjusted for typical Burmese preparation using moderate oil, wheat noodles, and chicken breast/thigh mix. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of noodle-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; wheat noodles cooked GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese-style fried noodles with chicken. GI of 50 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked wheat noodles and stir-fried noodle dishes (GI range 35–55 for wheat noodles; central estimate 50 used). GL per serving calculated as (50 × 18.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 32.4 g for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 70–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~50) and high glycemic load per serving (~32 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response, given the moderate GI and high per-serving glycemic load from wheat noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should be mindful of the combined sodium and saturated fat load per serving; choosing lean chicken breast and requesting less oil during preparation can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese-style fried noodles with chicken (ဆီချက်ခေါက်ဆွဲ ကြက်သား, romanized: Si Chet Khao Swe Kyat Tha) is a popular Myanmar noodle dish consisting of wheat-based egg noodles stir-fried in oil with chicken pieces, garlic, onion, soy sauce, fish sauce, and seasonal vegetables. The dish is cooked over high heat in a wok, producing noodles with a lightly charred, savory flavour and a tender-chewy texture. It is a staple comfort food in Myanmar and is widely served in Burmese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 578 kcal, 33 g protein, 22 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate, with notably high sodium (~1680 mg per serving) from soy sauce and fish sauce. The dish has a moderate glycemic index (~50) but a high glycemic load per serving due to the large noodle portion. Clinically, it warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GL), and cardiovascular disease (combined sodium and saturated fat). The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese-style fried noodles with chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central stir-fried noodle with chicken analogues and FAO/INFOODS SEA composites. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries. Confidence is medium due to the analogous estimation basis.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (stir-fried noodles with chicken, chow mein analogue) and FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle dish composites; no direct approved-source entry for this specific Burmese dish was found. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source databases for this dish category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source databases for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the reference source databases for this dish category. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA analogue source for home-style stir-fried noodle dishes). GI of 50 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entries; no direct GI measurement for this Burmese dish was found. Burmese cuisine does not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names under Veritas multilingual rules; those fields are null. The cuisine_tag 'Lor Mee' from the input source appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; this dish is not Lor Mee (a Hokkien braised noodle dish) and that tag has been disregarded.

Source is USDA FoodData Central stir-fried noodle with chicken analogue and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite, both approved sources. Source match type is 'estimated' with full explanation.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (50), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (32.4), and gi_source are all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate from wheat noodle entries. Estimation basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) used with gap note. FoodEx2 closest_parent A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) used with gap note. SNOMED CT is null with match_type 'none' and gap note. No codes invented; all match_type values are from the allowed set.
